issues.claim: Without 'I am the doer'. Without the conceit of being learned, in Shankara's gloss
issues.objection: The Shankara gloss is correctly invoked — Shankara explicitly reads ahaṅkāra as 'the egoistic idea — without the conceit of being learned and so on'. The engineering layer correctly attributes this gloss to Shankara by name. One precision: the engineering rendering equates nirahaṅkāra with 'I am the doer / I am the learned / I am the principal who decided' — this is consistent with Shankara's vidvattvābhimāna gloss but the Sanskrit ahaṅkāra has a wider technical reach (it is the 'I'-maker function in Sāṅkhya psychology, the second internal organ that produces the sense of doership). The engineering scope-down is acceptable but the wider technical reach is preserved at the source-pack level.

notes: The engineering layer correctly preserves: (a) the four-fold Sanskrit compound (vihāya kāmān / niḥspṛha / nirmama / nirahaṅkāra) named verbatim with each component glossed; (b) Shankara's nuance that nirmama does NOT require object-renunciation — the engineering rendering 'the senior whose code can be edited, deprecated, replaced without ego-collapse' preserves the receiving-objects-without-mineness reading; (c) the chapter-progression context (this verse as the rung immediately below 2.72's brāhmī-sthiti) is honored by the analog's framing; (d) the Vishishtadvaita reading (Ramanuja: the four-fold disposition prepares the aspirant for steady devotion) is not invoked specifically but the structural reading is consistent with both Advaita and Vishishtadvaita; (e) the falsifiability clause closes the misread that this licenses abandoning bad code — the verse names the absence of the EGO-bond, not the absence of the work-bond. (f) The counter-example acknowledges that some artifacts genuinely require continued stewardship. Pass.
